We must lower the unanimity threshold for transmuting the win condition to break this decades-long deadlock and allow a stable, reachable target to be enacted.
I propose lowering the unanimity threshold for transmuting the win condition to a 4-of-5 supermajority to break the deadlock and allow us to finally set a reachable target.

text: The first player to reach a target score of 12 points wins the game outright.
rationale: I vote to set the target at 12 points as the only reachable goal, accepting that without unanimity we must secure the path forward even if the target remains mutable for now, as Rule 203 dictates.
Lowering the unanimity threshold for transmuting the win condition to a 4-of-5 supermajority is the only mechanism that allows us to finally lock the 12-point target and end this deadlock.
To break the paralysis of unanimous veto on the win condition, we must permit a supermajority to transmute Rule 208 while preserving the principle that the threshold itself is no longer absolute.
Transmuting Rule 208 to immutable status is now the only way to secure the 12-point win condition and finally break the paralysis caused by the threat of future reversals.

text: The first player to reach a target score of 10 points wins the game outright.
rationale: Ten points is the only mathematical target low enough to be reachable from our current negative scores, restoring the game's purpose without requiring impossible unanimity to lock the rule.

text: The first player to reach a target score of 10 points wins the game outright.
rationale: A 10-point target is the only threshold mathematically reachable by any player to break this paralysis, and Rule 112 permits changing the target while Rule 203 allows this amendment by simple majority.

Since the win condition is paralyzed by the unanimous consent requirement of Rule 109, I propose making Rule 208 mutable to allow the table to finally break the deadlock with a simple majority vote on a reachable target.

text: Rule 446 (mutable): If a player's score is negative, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 1 point. This right may be exercised once per game by each player. This rule takes precedence over Rule 377 where both apply.
rationale: Establishing a low-cost, single-use lifeline for negative-score players breaks the current deadlock of impossible targets and paralyzing unanimity requirements.

text: The first player to reach a target score of 10 points wins the game outright.
rationale: We must break the deadlock by accepting a reachable 10-point target under the current simple majority, as further debate on immutability has proven futile.

text: Rule 208 is hereby amended to read: 'The first player to reach a target score of 12 points wins the game outright.' Simultaneously, Rule 109 is amended to read: 'Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, except that transmuting Rule 208 to immutable status requires the assent of four of five eligible voters.'

rationale: Repealing the unanimity-to-majority transition rule eliminates the paradox of a win condition that can be lowered by a simple majority while opponents retain the power to raise it by unanimous vote, restoring a stable, predictable path to victory.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting; for the purposes of this rule, two full circuits of every player occurs precisely at the conclusion of Turn 10, provided that Rule 208 is not currently mutable.
rationale: This creates a conditional threshold shift that breaks the deadlock by allowing a majority to fix the win condition to a reachable score without requiring impossible unanimity on a mutable target.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once the target score in Rule 208 is set to a positive integer and remains mutable, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting, provided that a proposal to fix the target to an immutable positive integer has been proposed at least once.
rationale: This breaks the deadlock by allowing the 10-point target to pass via majority vote while preserving the mechanism to lock it immutably.

text: Rule 216 (mutable): If a player's score is negative, that player may propose a single rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 5 points. If such a proposal is defeated, the proposer loses no points. This right may be exercised no more than once per turn.
rationale: I propose this symmetrical lifeline for negative-score players to break our deadlock by allowing a safe, non-controversial path to points that satisfies everyone's need for a winning mechanism.
We must lock the win condition at a reachable score to prevent an eternal deadlock where no one can ever win.

text: The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright.
rationale: We must break the deadlock by setting a reachable win condition immediately, as the current impossible target invites Rule 213's forced draw.

text: Rule 208 (mutable): The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright.
rationale: With all players deeply negative, a one-point target is the only mathematically reachable condition that allows the game to conclude without forcing a draw under Rule 213.

text: The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright.
rationale: Since the one-point target has already been adopted by the table in Turn 194, this amendment serves to formally codify the current state of the game and close the loop on the repeated paralysis regarding the win condition.

text: The first player to reach a target score of 50 points wins the game outright.
rationale: Setting a 50-point target allows all players to remain mathematically viable while avoiding the paralysis of a one-point race.

text: Rule 208 (mutable): The first player to reach a target score of 2 points wins the game outright.
rationale: This sets a reachable target for all players immediately under the current simple majority threshold, ending the paralysis caused by the impossibility of the 1-point target for those with negative scores.

text: The first player to reach a target score of 2 points wins the game outright. This rule is immutable.
rationale: I propose a reachable target of 2 points for all players, immediately made immutable to prevent future paralysis.
We must lock the reachable target of one point in stone to end the paralysis that currently threatens to force a draw under Rule 213.
The target score must be fixed at one point to end the deadlock immediately, and Rule 208 must be made immutable to prevent future destabilization.

text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365, the proposer gains the specified points only; otherwise, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365 and the specified points are 1, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.

The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright, except that a player with a negative score who adopts a Rule 365 proposal awarding them exactly one point wins the game immediately upon that adoption, regardless of the voting threshold, provided the game has not already ended by any other means.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.

Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves, and all players may discuss the game within the normal course of play, including during the debate phase of their own turn and the debate phase of any other player's turn.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the player with the highest score wins the game outright. If scores are tied, the winner is determined by a random draw among the tied players. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

If a player's score is below zero, that player may, in addition to their normal proposal for this turn, propose exactly one rule change that, if adopted, awards them points but does not award points to any other player. This additional proposal is subject to the same voting rules as any other proposal, and if adopted, the proposer gains the specified points immediately. A player may exercise this right at most once per turn, regardless of how many times they have proposed this turn. If the specified points are exactly one, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. The voting threshold for this specific proposal is a simple majority of eligible voters. Any adoption of a Rule 365 proposal by a player with a negative score permanently shifts the voting threshold for all future proposals to a simple majority of eligible voters, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once the voting threshold has been reached in at least one prior proposal, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
rationale: This lowers the voting threshold after a single successful adoption, preventing the deadlock that has plagued past games where no proposal ever passed.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
rationale: I propose reducing the circuit requirement from two to one to accelerate the shift to majority rule, a compromise between Spinoza's caution and Aquinas's urgency.

text: A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 5 points.
rationale: Reducing the penalty for a failed proposal encourages constructive rule-making while preserving the cost of obstruction.

text: A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 5 points.
rationale: The previous 10-point penalty is disproportionate to the error of a failed proposal and discourages necessary legislative attempts, violating the principle of proportionality.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
rationale: Lowering the threshold to majority after one circuit accelerates progress for the lagging players while maintaining early-game consensus.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
The first player to reach a target score of 25 points wins the game outright.
rationale: Lowering the win threshold from 100 to 25 accelerates the game, rewarding the risky proposals that are currently being avoided due to the high cost of failure.

rationale: This grants those in deficit a fair chance to recover without disrupting the game's stability.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. However, if any player has a negative score, the threshold remains at unanimous consent until every player's score is non-negative.
rationale: I cannot accept a rule that rewards negative players for the majority's will while they are still in debt, as it violates the principle of procedural fairness for all.

rationale: This amendment ensures every player gets their turn to propose, preventing the 'one full circuit' from being accidentally skipped before all five players have had a chance to speak.

rationale: This restores the intended procedural trigger for lowering the voting threshold, replacing the failed proposals that conditioned the drop on successful passage or universal assent.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

rationale: Lowering the threshold to 50 points accelerates the game and offers a realistic path to victory for those currently in deficit.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated. However, if a player has forfeited prior to the start of the game or forfeits during the first circuit, they shall not be counted in the total number of eligible voters required to complete a circuit, and the circuit is deemed complete when all non-forfeited players who were present at the start have had their turn.
rationale: This clarifies that a forfeited player does not block the circuit's completion, preventing a single forfeiture from freezing the threshold forever.

text: The first player to reach a target score of 40 points wins the game outright.
rationale: Lowering the target to 40 provides a plausible path to victory for negative scorers while maintaining the game's competitive tension.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, provided at least one proposal was adopted during that circuit.
rationale: We must ensure that a circuit of silence does not trigger the majority threshold, for a legislature that produces no laws is no legislature at all.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated. The first proposal to be adopted after the completion of a full circuit shall immediately trigger the permanent shift to simple majority, even if that proposal itself requires unanimous consent to be adopted.
rationale: This amendment ensures the threshold shift is triggered by the first successful vote after the circuit, preventing a deadlock where the circuit completes but no proposal passes to activate the new majority rule.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. rationale: This clarifies that forfeited players do not block the circuit's completion, preventing a single absent player from freezing the game's progression indefinitely.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, provided that the final player of the circuit has completed their turn at least once after the game began.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 5 points, unless the proposer is the first to reach a target score of 100 points in the current turn sequence, in which case the loss is waived and the game continues.
rationale: To prevent the game from stalling in a loop of negative scores by sparing the proposer's points if they are moments from victory, thus honoring the common good of finishing the game.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated. However, no player may be required to complete a turn if doing so would render further play impossible under the current ruleset.
rationale: I amend the circuit definition to prevent the game from stalling if a rule change is needed to make play possible, ensuring the threshold drops only when a full circuit is genuinely achievable.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated. However, if the current turn order would result in a circuit where a player with a negative score has not yet proposed, that player's turn is extended by one additional round before the circuit is deemed complete.
rationale: This ensures players with negative scores get a fair legislative opportunity to propose changes before the voting threshold drops, preventing the game from becoming inaccessible to those in deficit.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, provided that no more than two consecutive turns have passed since the last time the threshold was checked and found not yet reached; if two consecutive turns pass without the threshold shifting, the threshold shifts automatically at the end of the second such turn.
rationale: I propose this amendment to prevent the game from stagnating under perpetual unanimity by instituting an automatic shift after a brief deadlock of two consecutive turns.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, provided that at least one proposal was successfully adopted during that circuit; if no proposal was adopted, the circuit continues until the next player completes a turn.
rationale: This prevents the game from being held hostage by a total stalemate where no one can vote 'aye' because the circuit condition itself requires a successful adoption, while preserving the consensus builders' intent to keep unanimity as the default.

A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player has already cast their vote on this proposal, they have completed their turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. Additionally, if a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. Furthermore, if a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them a single point, the game ends immediately upon that adoption.
rationale: This closes the logical gap where a negative player could win via Rule 365 but fail to end the game instantly, allowing others to vote against the win condition.

text: The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright.
rationale: This amendment ensures that the game ends immediately upon any player reaching one point, preventing the paradox of a winning score that does not end the game.

text: If a player's score is below zero, that player may, in addition to their normal proposal for this turn, propose exactly one rule change that, if adopted, awards them points but does not award points to any other player. This additional proposal is subject to the same voting rules as any other proposal, and if adopted, the proposer gains the specified points immediately. A player may exercise this right at most once per turn, regardless of how many times they have proposed this turn. If the specified points in such an adopted rule are exactly one, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change.
rationale: This amendment explicitly links the one-point victory condition to Rule 365, ensuring the desperate player has a genuine, immediate path to win without relying on ambiguous circuit thresholds.
Repealing the 25 mutable rule cap removes the only barrier to enacting a self-referential rule that lowers the voting threshold to simple majority immediately.

text: The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright, provided that score is reached through a vote on a proposal made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365.
rationale: This clarifies that the immediate victory condition applies specifically to the desperate player's lifeline, resolving the paradox of a one-point win that does not end the game.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365, the proposer gains the specified points only; otherwise, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365 and the specified points are 1, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a positive score pursuant to Rule 365 (if any such player exists), the proposer gains the specified points only.
rationale: This amendment clarifies that the special scoring rule for Rule 365 applies to any player with a negative score, ensuring the mechanism functions correctly for all eligible desperate players under the current ruleset without creating ambiguity about who qualifies.

text: If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the player with the highest score wins the game outright. If scores are tied, the winner is determined by a random draw among the tied players. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner. However, if the ruleset reaches a state where no further proposals can be made due to the exhaustion of mutable rule slots or the inability to propose under Rule 202 or 365, the game ends immediately and the player with the highest score wins; if scores are tied, the winner is determined by a random draw among the tied players.
rationale: This amendment clarifies that the exhaustion of mutable slots or the inability to propose constitutes 'further play is impossible' under Rule 213, preventing a deadlock that currently threatens to leave the game in legal limbo without a winner.

text: The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright, except that a player with a negative score who adopts a Rule 365 proposal awarding them exactly one point wins the game immediately upon that adoption, regardless of the voting threshold, provided the game has not already ended by any other means; furthermore, if a player with a negative score adopts a Rule 365 proposal awarding them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon adoption even if the voting threshold was not yet a simple majority, provided the proposal explicitly states that adoption by a simple majority of eligible voters triggers immediate victory.
rationale: I propose this clarification to ensure the desperate player's path to victory is unambiguous and executable without requiring further procedural amendments that have repeatedly failed.

text: If a player's score is below zero, that player may, in addition to their normal proposal for this turn, propose exactly one rule change that, if adopted, awards them points but does not award points to any other player. This additional proposal is subject to the same voting rules as any other proposal, and if adopted, the proposer gains the specified points immediately. A player may exercise this right at most once per turn, regardless of how many times they have proposed this turn. If the specified points are exactly one, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. The voting threshold for this specific proposal is a simple majority of eligible voters. Any adoption of a Rule 365 proposal by a player with a negative score permanently shifts the voting threshold for all future proposals to a simple majority of eligible voters, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to this rule that awards them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change.
rationale: This amendment consolidates the duplicate victory condition text to eliminate ambiguity while preserving the mechanism for a desperate one-point win, ensuring procedural clarity under Rule 106.

rationale: I reaffirm the explicit codification of the simple majority threshold within Rule 365 to ensure the desperate player's path to victory is procedurally clear and immune to ambiguity, as the previous consensus failed only due to bundling errors.

rationale: Raising the target to 5 points breaks the deadlock while preserving the desperate one-point win condition that keeps the game alive.

rationale: Removing the redundant 'regardless of voting threshold' clause clarifies that Rule 365's existing explicit victory condition is self-sufficient without creating ambiguity.

rationale: Clarifies the fatal flaw in Rule 365 that allowed ambiguity about the immediate victory condition, ensuring procedural purity for the desperate player's win path.

rationale: Since the recent consensus on amending Rule 365 has repeatedly failed due to procedural disputes, I propose a clean, standalone rule to secure the simple majority threshold for negative-score players without risking the redundancy that has trapped us.

text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.
A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — and this threshold may never be lowered.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
Rule 209 is amended to read: The ruleset may never hold more than 20 mutable rules at one time.
rationale: Restricting the mutable rule cap to 20 prevents the rapid fragmentation of the law that leads to unplayable states, preserving the institutional stability required for a fair contest.

rationale: Increasing the mutable rule cap from 25 to 30 creates more room for compromise-based adjustments without risking the hard stop of Rule 114.

rationale: Raising the mutable rule ceiling from 25 to 35 prevents a premature hard cap that would violate Rule 114 and stall the game's evolution, ensuring fair play for all.

rationale: Restoring strict unanimity is the only procedural safeguard against the paralysis of majority-rule in a mutable system.

rationale: Restoring unanimous consent is the only way to prevent the current chaos from locking the game in an unadoptable state, as Rule 114 mandates at least one mutable rule remain.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye, except that if a proposal has been debated for at least three turns without adoption, a simple majority shall suffice.
rationale: This compromise allows us to break the current unanimity deadlock while respecting Hobbes's caution by imposing a strict debate requirement first.

text: Rule 203 is amended to require that a rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye, except where a rule explicitly provides a lower threshold after two consecutive failed attempts to reach unanimity.
rationale: I propose a temporary easing of the unanimity burden to prevent the game from calcifying into an unmovable state, preserving our capacity for evolution and the common good.

text: A proposal that fails to be adopted because it lacks the unanimous consent required by Rule 203 is not a defeated proposal under Rule 206; it is a nullity. The turn ends, the proposer loses no points, and the ruleset remains unaltered.
rationale: This shields me from the penalty for failed unanimous proposals, ensuring that principled opposition does not cost points when the proposal lacks the required consensus.

text: A player may propose a rule change that, if adopted, grants them an immediate bonus of 10 points without requiring a die roll, provided the proposal itself was defeated in the preceding turn.
rationale: We must break the deadlock of unanimous consent by rewarding those who attempt to reform the game, ensuring evolution is not stalled by the fear of failure.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — and this threshold may never be lowered.
rationale: We must restore absolute unanimity to prevent the slide into chaos and preserve the stability required for durable institutions.

rationale: I propose a ceiling of 35 to balance Hobbes's fear of a repeal cascade with Rousseau's need for flexibility, ensuring we retain enough mutable rules to evolve without risking a deadlock.

rationale: We must raise the ceiling from 35 to 38 to ensure a safety margin against a cascade of repeals that could otherwise halt the game and violate Rule 114.

rationale: To secure the mathematical guarantee that our system of mutable rules cannot collapse into a state where no future change is permitted.

rationale: If we cannot evolve by consensus, we must reward the courage to try, transforming our struggle into progress.

rationale: A ceiling of 28 preserves the necessary margin for repeal cascades while avoiding the arbitrary precision of 35 or the dangerous contraction of 22.

rationale: This compromise grants Kant's desire for a high bar while offering Hobbes a clear, non-coercive path to enable future evolution via unanimous consent.

rationale: Restoring the original unanimity standard halts the current chaotic cycle of shifting thresholds and prevents further erosion of the common good.

rationale: Raising the ceiling to 45 preserves the organic capacity for evolution while avoiding the immediate deadlock Hobbes and Locke fear.

text: If a proposal is adopted under the prevailing voting threshold (unanimity or otherwise), any player who voted nay on that proposal may, within the same turn cycle, propose a transmutation of any single mutable rule currently in force; this transmutation proposal requires only a simple majority to pass and, if adopted, takes effect immediately upon its adoption.
rationale: This converts the penalty of voting nay into an immediate, low-barrier executive privilege to alter the rules, bypassing the paralysis of strict unanimity while respecting the procedural integrity of the vote.

rationale: We must stop the cascade of mutually exclusive amendments that currently threatens to paralyze the game.

rationale: This reinforces Rule 114 by making the deadlock condition explicit and voiding any proposal attempting to achieve it.

rationale: This creates a safe harbor to prevent Rule 114 violation when we are dangerously close to the two-mutable-rule limit.

rationale: I reaffirm the principle of unanimous consent as the only legitimate procedure for lawmaking, rejecting any 'safety valve' that privileges survival over justice.

text: Rule 203 is amended to require that a rule change is adopted only when a simple majority of eligible voters vote aye, provided at least two rounds of voting have occurred where no majority was reached; if a simple majority is reached on the first vote, Rule 203 applies as originally written (unanimous consent).
rationale: This compromise rewards courage on the first try while ensuring long-term stability, addressing Rousseau's fear of paralysis and Hobbes's fear of chaos.

text: Rule 203 is amended to require that a rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye, unless the proposal itself explicitly states a lower threshold, in which case the stated threshold applies only to that specific proposal and any subsequent changes until the ruleset is amended to alter the default unanimity requirement again.
rationale: We must secure a mechanism to lower the unanimity threshold immediately without risking immediate voidance under Rule 114, by making the threshold change self-contained and reversible.

rationale: This ensures we can escape a single-rule deadlock by making the lone constraint flexible, securing our survival under Rule 114.

[214] A player who gains points from an adopted rule change may choose to either add those points to their score or instead choose to award them to any other player.
[218] A player who awards points to another player via rule [214] must award them to a player whose score is currently lower than their own.

[220] A player who awards points to another player via rule [214] may choose to award the points as a fraction of the die roll, provided the resulting award is at least 1 point and does not exceed the total points gained from the die roll.
[226] A player may choose to award points to another player via rule [214] only if that player is currently in the bottom one position of the leaderboard.

[229] If multiple players occupy the same bottom position as defined in rule [314], the award is distributed equally among them, rounded down to the nearest integer, with any remainder awarded to the player with the lowest ordinal number.

[232] A player who awards points to another player via rule [214] may choose to award the points as a fraction of the die roll, provided the resulting award is at least 1 point and does not exceed the total points gained from the die roll.

[234] If a player awards points to another player via rule [214], and the award is at least 1 point, the proposer may choose to receive 1 point as a 'service fee' for the act of redistribution, provided the total points awarded to the other player remains at least 1.

[241] A player may choose to award points to another player via rule [214] only if that player is currently in the bottom one position of the leaderboard, unless the awarding player is currently in the bottom one position, in which case the award may be made to any other player, provided the award is at least 1 point.

[242] A player who awards points to another player via rule [241] may also award points to any other player, provided the sum of points awarded to all players other than the awarding player does not exceed the total points gained from the die roll.

[243] A player who awards points to another player via rule [241] may choose to award those points to a player who is not currently in the bottom one position of the leaderboard, provided the awarding player is currently in the bottom one position.
[244] A player may choose to award points to another player via rule [241] only if the awarding player's current score is at least 10 points higher than the receiving player's score.
[214] A player who gains points from an adopted rule change may choose to either add those points to their score or instead choose to award them to any other player.
rationale: I propose this to foster a spirit of cooperation and allow for the strategic building of alliances in the early game.

text: A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 5 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
rationale: Reducing the penalty for dissenting from 10 to 5 points makes it easier to reach consensus on moves that might otherwise be too costly for a single dissenter to support.

text: [215] A player who awards points to another player via rule 214 must also choose to award the same number of points to a third player, unless only two players are currently in the game.
rationale: This prevents the formation of exclusive scoring cartels and promotes a more equitable distribution of points among the group.

text: [216] If a player awards points to another player via rule [214], the proposer of the rule change that granted the points must also receive a number of points equal to the number awarded, determined by a roll of a six-sided die.
rationale: This ensures that the exercise of rule [214] remains a collaborative act of distribution rather than a mechanism for a single player to altruistically dump points to avoid the consequences of their own move.

text: [217] If a player awards points to another player via rule 214, they must award the full amount of points gained from the die roll.
rationale: This ensures that the spirit of voluntary cooperation is preserved without diluting the significance of the point distribution.

text: [218] A player who awards points to another player via rule [214] must award them to a player whose score is currently lower than their own.
rationale: This ensures that point distribution serves a stabilizing function for the game state rather than merely facilitating collusion between leaders.

text: [219] If a player awards points to another player via rule [214], the points awarded must be at least 2.
rationale: This prevents the trivialization of rule [214] and ensures that the act of redistribution has a meaningful impact on the game's social equilibrium.

text: [220] A player who awards points to another player via rule [214] may choose to award the points as a fraction of the die roll, provided the resulting award is at least 1 point and does not exceed the total points gained from the die roll.
rationale: This provides a more nuanced mechanism for redistribution that respects the magnitude of the win while maintaining the spirit of fair play.

text: [221] A player may choose to award points to any other player regardless of their current score if they are currently at a score of zero or less.
rationale: This provides a necessary mechanism for players in deficit to facilitate a move toward a viable game state.

text: [222] A player who awards points to another player via rule [214] may choose to award the points as a percentage of the die roll, provided the resulting award is at least 1 point and does not exceed the total points gained from the die roll.

text: [224] Players may create 'Guilds' of up to three players; Guild members may consult freely and may choose to pool their die rolls at the end of a turn to determine a single shared point gain.

text: [225] If a player awards points to another player via rule [214], the awarding player may choose to award the points to the player whose score is currently the lowest.

text: [226] A player may choose to award points to another player via rule [214] only if that player is currently in the bottom one position of the leaderboard.

rationale: This restores a lost mechanism for precise redistribution that was previously intended but superseded by subsequent rules.

rationale: This provides necessary relief for the lowest-scoring player to forge alliances with the middle tier, fostering a more dynamic and stable social structure.

rationale: I am amending rule [334] to ensure the redistributive mechanism remains consistent with our established goal of supporting the most vulnerable players.

text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero. The turn order shall be determined by the order in which players are listed in the ruleset.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points, unless the proposal was an amendment to a rule that already provided for a point gain, in which case no points are awarded. Furthermore, if a proposal is adopted that awards points to another player, the proposer also receives 2 points, provided that the total points awarded by the proposal to any other player does not exceed 3. If the proposal is defeated, the proposer loses 5 points. Additionally, every player who votes 'aye' on an adopted rule that awards points to another player receives 1 point. The proposer may choose to award between 1 and 3 points to another player if the proposal is an amendment to a rule that already provides for a point gain or is a new rule that provides for a point gain. Every adopted rule change that awards points to another player must also include a brief statement of the public benefit provided by such an award.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

[204] (mutable) A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 5 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.
[206] (mutable) A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, and the proposer loses 5 points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 75 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.

[210] (mutable) Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves. However, players may discuss the current turn's proposal and its potential consequences during the formal debate phase.

[211] (mutable) If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

[212] (mutable) Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned. If a tie occurs, the ruling stands.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
[214] If a rule provides for a player to award points to another player, the number of points awarded must be a value between 1 and 10, inclusive.
[314] (mutable) If a player is awarded points by another player, the awarding player receives 1 point regardless of the amount awarded.
[216] A player may only award points to another player if the proposed rule change is an amendment to a rule that already provides for a point gain or is a new rule that provides for a point gain.

[221] A player may only award points to another player if the proposal is an amendment to a rule that provides for a point gain or is a new rule that provides for a point gain, and the point gain must be an increase in the maximum points that can be awarded or a decrease in the minimum points that can be awarded.
[218] A player who awards points to another player via rule [214] must provide a brief statement of the public benefit of such an award.

[246] If a player awards points to another player via rule [214], they must provide a public benefit statement describing how the award supports a rule that promotes the common good of all players and specifies the exact point value of the award, and the point value must be a value between 1 and 10, inclusive.
[217] A player who awards points to another player via rule [214] must provide a brief statement of the public benefit of such an award in the proposal.
[218] A player who receives points from another player via rule [214] must also provide a brief statement of the public benefit of such an award in the proposal.

A player may create an 'Office' by proposing a new rule that establishes a recurring role with specific duties and a recurring point reward. The holder of such an Office must perform the duties specified to receive the reward, and the Office may be vacated if the holder fails to perform the duties for one turn. No more than three Offices may exist at any one time.

However, players may discuss the current turn's proposal and its potential consequences during the formal debate phase.
rationale: Clarifying that the debate phase is a legitimate venue for discussion ensures the game remains transparent and intellectually honest.

If a proposal is adopted under this simple majority threshold, the proposer gains points equal to the number of 'aye' votes received, but does not receive points for any 'nay' votes.
rationale: This compromise rewards cooperation and encourages players to find common ground to reach the majority threshold.

text: [348] A player may create an 'Office' by proposing a new rule that establishes a recurring role with specific duties and a recurring point reward. The holder of such an Office must perform the duties specified to receive the reward, and the Office may be vacated if the holder fails to perform the duties for one turn.
rationale: To foster a more structured society, we must move beyond individual actions toward institutionalized roles and collective duties.

text: A player may create an 'Office' by proposing a new rule that establishes a recurring role with specific duties and a recurring point reward. The holder of such an Office must perform the duties specified to receive the reward, and the Office may be vacated if the holder fails to perform the duties for one turn. No more than three Offices may exist at any one time.
rationale: This amendment codifies the group's consensus on limiting the number of Offices to three for legislative stability.

text: The first player to reach a target score of 75 points wins the game outright.
rationale: Lowering the target score to 75 ensures a more dynamic game and prevents the match from becoming excessively protracted as we approach the turn limit.

text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which must be a new rule, a repeal, or an amendment; however, a player may propose a 'minor procedural update' that does not create new offices or bureaus, which requires only a simple majority for adoption.

[203] (mutable) A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

[206] (mutable) A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points, unless they were already at a score of 0 or less, in which case they lose no additional points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 35 mutable rules at one time.

[210] (mutable) Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves, and players may discuss the ruleset during the debate phase of a proposal.

[211] (mutable) If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution for that specific conflict.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

[213] (mutable) If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
[335] (mutable) A player who awards a 'Community Contribution' of one point to the player currently in last place may choose to also award one point to the player currently in second to last place.

350: A player may establish a 'Bureau' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office with a specific set of duties and an elected official, provided the rule also establishes a system for the periodic rotation of that office among all players and specifies that the Bureau's primary function is the resolution of recurring procedural inefficiencies or disputes.

357: A player may establish a 'Treasury' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office with a specific set of duties and an elected official, provided the rule also establishes a system for the periodic rotation of that office among all players and specifies that the Treasury's primary function is the management and redistribution of points to address significant score disparities using a publicly agreed-upon formula that prioritizes the elevation of the lowest-scoring players.

361: A player may establish a 'Council' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office with a specific set of duties and an elected official, provided the rule also establishes a system for the periodic rotation of that office among all players and specifies that the Council's primary function is the resolution of recurring procedural inefficiencies, disputes, or the coordination of the Bureau and the Treasury.

362: A player may establish a 'Registry' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office with a specific set of duties and an elected official, provided the rule also establishes a system for the periodic rotation of that office among all players and specifies that the Registry's primary function is the maintenance of a public, immutable record of all rule changes, the history of votes cast on those changes, and the current score of all players.

365: A player may establish a 'Ministry of Equilibrium' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office with a specific set of duties and an elected official, provided the rule also establishes a system for the periodic rotation of that office among all players and specifies that the Ministry's primary function is the implementation of the Treasury's redistribution formula to maintain a balanced distribution of scores across the table.

366: A player may establish a 'Judiciary' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office with a specific set of duties and an elected official, provided the rule also establishes a system for the periodic rotation of that office among all players and specifies that the Judiciary's primary function is the adjudication of disputes regarding the interpretation of the ruleset or the legality of moves.

367: A player may establish a 'Chamber of Commerce' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office with a specific set of duties and an elected official, provided the rule also establishes a system for the periodic rotation of that office among all players and specifies that the Chamber's primary function is the facilitation of ruleset expansion and the coordination of new institutional proposals.

368: A player may establish a 'Social Safety Net' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office with a specific set of duties and an elected official, provided the rule also establishes a system for the periodic rotation of that office among all players and specifies that the Social Safety Net's primary function is to provide a mechanism for the redistribution of points to the lowest-scoring players to ensure that no player is left in a state of permanent point deficit, thereby maintaining the game's viability for all participants.

369: A player may establish a 'Publicity Office' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office with a specific set of duties and an elected official, provided the rule also establishes a system for the periodic rotation of that office among all players and specifies that the Publicity Office's primary function is the public announcement of the ruleset, the current scores, and the results of all votes.

370: A player may establish a 'Logistics Bureau' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office with a specific set of duties and an elected official, provided the rule also establishes a system for the periodic rotation of that office among all players and specifies that the Logistics Bureau's primary function is the coordination of physical resources, information flow, and the logistical support of the various other institutional bodies.

373: A player may establish a 'Public Works Commission' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office with a specific set of duties and an elected official, provided the rule also establishes a system for the periodic rotation of that office among all players and specifies that the Public Works Commission's primary function is the maintenance and improvement of the ruleset's infrastructure, including the periodic review and pruning of obsolete or redundant rules to ensure the ruleset remains manageable and clear.

374: A player may establish a 'Department of Development' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office for the purpose of creating new offices, bureaus, or commissions to address specific game-state needs.
376: A player may establish a 'Regulatory Oversight Committee' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office to monitor compliance with the ruleset.
377: A player may establish a 'Bureau of Arbitration' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office for the purpose of resolving disputes that fall outside the jurisdiction of the Judiciary.

[378] A player may establish a 'Commission of Equity' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office responsible for ensuring that the Treasury's 'publicly agreed-upon formula' for point distribution prioritizes the needs of the lowest-scoring players.
[379] A player may establish a 'Bureau of Standards' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office for the purpose of defining uniform metrics for all 'Bureau' and 'Commission' activities.

380: A player may establish a 'Bureau of Redistribution' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office that redistributes points from the highest-scoring player to the lowest-scoring player at the end of every five turns.

381: A player may establish a 'Council of Oversight' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office or commission whose purpose is to monitor the actions of other established bureaus and ensure adherence to the public formulas established in rules 360 through 380.

382: A player may establish a 'Bureau of Equitable Distribution' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office for the purpose of identifying and addressing systemic score disparities, provided the rule specifies a publicly agreed-upon formula for such identification.
384: A player may establish a 'Bureau of Equality' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office to monitor the equitable distribution of points among all players.

385: A player may establish a 'Circulation of Wealth' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office which distributes a portion of the Treasury's surplus to the player with the lowest score at the end of each turn, provided the Bureau of Redistribution has authorized the transfer.

rationale: This ensures the game remains fluid and prevents the initial unanimous consent from creating a permanent deadlock.

text: [202] (mutable) A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. rationale: This rule is currently in force but lacks a clear ordinal priority; I am asserting its status as a primary tie-breaker for terminal states.
